topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a relentless, irritating drone that seemed to amplify the frantic beating of my own heart. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, the screen a blinding expanse of accusation. Every word I typed felt like a stone being dropped into a deep, dark well, and the echo of the impending consequence vibrated in my bones. Professor Albright’s email sat open on my second monitor, the stark black text mocking my attempts to formulate a defense. I reread the accusation—the specific passage, the undeniable similarity—and a cold, clammy sweat prickled my hairline. I scrubbed my face with the back of my hand, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. I wanted to scream, to dismantle the unfair structure of this situation, but the sound caught in my throat, reduced to a dry, rasping silence. I looked around at the hushed students, their focused expressions seeming to sharpen into judging slits, and I felt a profound sense of isolation, as if I were submerged beneath an ocean of scrutiny.

[story 2]

"Just tell me what you did," the dean’s voice cut through the sterile air, sharp and unforgiving. I kept my hands clasped tightly beneath the table, the knuckles white against the worn wood. The room seemed to shrink, the walls pressing in, the air growing thin and difficult to draw into my lungs. I looked down at my hands, noticing the tremor that had started in my fingertips and now ran up my arms, a visible sign of the internal upheaval. Every glance from a passing colleague felt like a physical prod, each one a tiny spike of pressure against my chest. My mouth was dry; I tried to speak, to offer a reasoned explanation, but the words dissolved into a pathetic whisper. I just stared at the floor tiles, watching a small crack in the grout, wishing with a desperate intensity that I could simply vanish into the beige carpet.

[story 3]

The silence in my cramped dorm room was suffocating, heavy enough to lean on. I paced the small perimeter of the rug, my movements becoming circular, repetitive, fueled by a nervous energy that wouldn't subside. Each time I stopped, I would run a hand over my face, feeling the tension coil tight beneath my skin. The evidence lay spread across my desk—the highlighted source material, the damning comparison—and it felt like a physical weight anchoring me to the spot. I picked up a pen, the plastic cool and slick in my grip, but the ink refused to flow onto the paper. I just kept turning the pen over and over, the repetitive motion a desperate attempt to impose some order on the swirling chaos inside my head. The door handle felt impossibly far away, and the thought of facing the inevitable confrontation made my stomach clench into a hard, painful knot.
